text_blocks.text: Fossils found in Kenya suggest that primates
roamed the area more than 20 million years ago.
Recent findings near Lake Turkana indicate that
hominids such as Homo habilis (1.8 and 2.5 million
years ago) and Homo erectus (1.8 million to 350,000
years ago) are possible direct ancestors of modern
Homo sapiens, and lived in Kenya in the Pleistocene
epoch. During excavations at Lake Turkana in 1984,
paleoanthropologist Richard Leakey assisted by Kamoya
Kimeu discovered the Turkana Boy, a
Previous research on early hominids is particularly
identified with Mary Keakey and Louis Leakey, who
were responsible for the preliminary archaeological
research at Ologesailie and Hyrax Hill. Later work
at the former site was undertaken by Glynn Isaac.
